Okay, I'll start by exploring the project to understand its structure and how to run it. Then, I'll create a plan to improve the Zero Computer based on user feedback. First up, `package.json` to check dependencies and
scripts.

`package.json` shows a Node.js/Express backend with WebSockets for real-time communication, and a Vite/React frontend. Browser automation is handled by Playwright, and `open` is used for file/URL access. The `dev` and `start` scripts both launch the server at `server/index
.js`, while `build` compiles the React app. I'll now inspect `server/index.js` to understand server setup, frontend interaction, and automation.

`server/index.js` is the core. It runs an Express server for APIs and the frontend, using WebSockets for real-time updates. The key API is `/api/v2/projects` for persistent project management. `projectExecutor.js` handles project execution, and there's a `/api/v
2/projects/zero` endpoint for self-editing the codebase. I'll now examine the frontend, starting with `web/src/App.jsx`, to understand user interaction.
`App.jsx` shows a typical React app using WebSockets for real-time task updates. For my goal, the key is the `ProjectsV2` component, which I'll now examine in `web/src/components/Projects
V2.jsx` to understand project management in the UI.

`ProjectsV2.jsx` is the project UI. I can create a new project or an "Edit Zero" project to modify the app's code, which I will use. The UI displays project lists and a detailed view with status, logs, and checkpoints, all updated in real-time via WebSockets. My

goal is to improve the app. I'll start by adding a "Copy to Clipboard" button for project logs. I've reviewed `ProjectsV2.jsx` and will modify the `ProjectDetail` component. I'll add the button, implement `navigator.clipboard.writeText()` for copying, and

the component's full context.

.writeText`. A "Copy" button will be added to the terminal header, and a "Copied!" message will briefly appear on success.
I've added "Copy to Clipboard" to `ProjectsV2.jsx`, so now I'll style it in `web/src/styles/index.css`. I'll style `.terminal-header`, `.terminal-
actions`, and `.copy-status`. First, I'll check `index.css` for existing styles.

I will now create the necessary Kotlin source
files for the application: `ZeroApp.kt` for initialization, `AgentService.kt` for the background service, and `MainActivity.kt` for the user interface.

I will now correct the project structure by creating the `Theme.kt` file in the appropriate directory to define the `ZeroAgentTheme`. Then, I will modify `MainActivity.kt` to remove the incorrect package declaration and inline theme definition,
ensuring it correctly imports the theme from the new file.
I will update `AgentService.kt` to ensure compatibility with Android 14 by explicitly including

the `ServiceInfo.FOREGROUND_SERVICE_TYPE_DATA_SYNC` parameter in the `startForeground` call when running on API level 29 or higher. This aligns the code with the service type declared in the manifest.
I will now create a `README.md` file to document the project. This file will explain
the application's features, prerequisites (specifically noting the absence of the Gradle wrapper), and provide instructions on how to build and run the app.
I will list the contents of the root directory and the `app/src/main/java/com/zerocomputer/agent
` directory to confirm that all project files and the source code have been correctly created.
📍 Created
a complete Native Android Project (Kotlin + Compose).
- Implemented `AgentService
` as a Foreground Service with type `dataSync` (Android 14 compliant).
- Implemented `MainActivity` with Jetpack Compose UI to control
the service.
- Implemented Notification logic with real-time progress bar updates.
- Added `AndroidManifest.xml` with necessary permissions (`POST_NOTIFICATIONS`,
`FOREGROUND_SERVICE`).
- Added standard Gradle build configuration.
The app is ready to be opened in Android Studio and built.
